fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that input validation library we've been talking about. The idea is that instead of writing a bunch of scattered if-checks all over the place, devs can just declare what they expect from a value and get back a consistent result. Think of it like the assertion approach we used in that old form-handling module — same kind of vibe but more structured and reusable.

The engine needs to handle all the usual suspects: type checking, range/length stuff, format validation (emails, URLs, dates, that sort of thing), and some collection-level checks. There also needs to be a way to short-circuit on the first failure vs. collecting all failures at once — both patterns are needed depending on the use case.

Results should be machine-readable and stable so downstream systems can react to specific failure types without parsing human text. The codes need to be consistent across runs — can't be changing them.

Also important: the architecture can't just be one big file. We've been burned by that before. Keep the parsing, the rule logic, and the output formatting cleanly separated.

One thing I'm fuzzy on is how null values should interact with rules — like, should a null automatically fail everything, or can we make certain rules tolerant of nulls? Need to nail that down. Same question for applying a rule across a whole list of values at once. Talk to the team about the exact behaviors there.

A couple more specifics from the team convo so we lock this down cleanly. The violation codes are not just generally stable, they are fixed and must never change. The exact mapping is: float=9, integer=10, digit=11, integerish=12, boolean=13, not_empty=14, not_null=15, string=16, regex=17, min_length=18, max_length=19, starts_with=20, contains=21, in_choices=22, numeric=23, is_array=24, null=25, key_exists=26, not_blank=27, uuid (no alias)=40, range=30, alnum=31, is_true=32, equal=33, same=34, min=35, max=36, length=37, is_false=38, ends_with=39, not_equal=42, not_same=43, not_in_choices=47, email=201, url=203, empty=205, json_string=206, scalar=209, less_than=210, less_or_equal=211, greater_than=212, greater_or_equal=213, date=214, key_not_exists=216.

On the null question, we do have a definite behavior now: when the request carries `null_or: true` alongside a check, the engine must short-circuit to valid immediately if the value is null, without invoking the underlying rule at all. Only non-null values are forwarded to the rule for evaluation. This applies to any rule and should be handled as wrapper behavior in the adapter/dispatch layer, not inside individual rules.

Also worth being explicit about the comparison rules because people asked. `equal` (code=33) performs a type-coercing comparison, so string `"10"` equals integer `10`. `same` (code=34) requires both value and type to match strictly. In the same spirit, `not_equal` (code=42) fails when values are loosely equal, and `not_same` (code=43) fails when values are strictly identical.

For collection handling, when the request carries `all: true`, the value is expected to be a collection (array). The named rule is applied to each element in order. The check stops at the first element that fails and returns that element's violation, using the same code and message format as a single-value check. If all elements pass, the result is valid.

For `chain` mode, the request supplies a `value` and a `steps` array where each step is `{check, args}`. Steps are evaluated left to right against the same value. Execution halts at the first failing step and returns that violation. If all steps pass, the result is valid. And if a step references a rule name that does not exist, the output must be exactly two lines: `error=unknown_assertion` followed by `message=Assertion "<ruleName>" does not exist.`

On structure, keeping this split up is a hard requirement, not just a preference. We need (1) a core engine module containing rule definitions and a ViolationError type with code+message, fully decoupled from stdin/stdout/JSON, (2) a rule registry for resolving rule names to implementations, (3) an I/O adapter that reads JSON from stdin, routes to the engine, and renders the stdout contract, and (4) an output formatter. New rules must be addable without modifying existing rule files, so we should stick to the Open/Closed Principle here.

Last thing, this should follow the declarative rule-based check/chain/lazy dispatch pattern described in the Input/Output Contract section of start.md, specifically the three execution modes and their JSON request schemas, and that includes the `null_or: true` flag on a check-mode request which makes any rule skip evaluation and return valid when value is null.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: check(ruleName: string, value: any, args: any[]): ValidationResult

trigger_keywords: lazy mode
aggregate all failures
collect all errors
batch validation
report every failure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In `lazy` mode, the request has an `assertions` array. Each assertion is `{value, property_path, steps}`. All assertions are evaluated independently (each using chain-style step sequencing internally). Every failure is collected. If there are N failures the output begins with `The following <N> assertions failed:` followed by numbered lines `<i>) <property_path>: <message>` — one per failure, in order. If zero failures occur, output is simply `valid\n`.

trigger_keywords: error message format
how is the violation message worded
message template
what does the invalid output look like
output format for failure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A violated rule always outputs exactly three lines: line 1 is `invalid`, line 2 is `code=<integer>`, line 3 is `message=<text>`. The message text quotes the offending value in double-quotes. Special values are represented as tokens: null appears as `<NULL>`, boolean true as `<TRUE>`, boolean false as `<FALSE>`, and arrays as `<ARRAY>`. A satisfied rule outputs only `valid\n`.

trigger_keywords: integerish rule
castable to integer
string that looks like integer
integer-like string
numeric string integer
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `integerish` rule (code=12) accepts native integers AND strings whose entire content represents a whole number with no fractional part — e.g. `"10"` passes. Floats like `5.5`, non-numeric strings, booleans, and null all fail. The error message is: `Value "<val>" is not an integer or a number castable to integer.`

trigger_keywords: between_length vs min_length message
length range error message
between_length error
min_length code same as between_length
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: `between_length` and `min_length` share the same violation code (18) for the too-short case. However the messages differ: `min_length` says `it should have more than <n> characters` while `between_length` says `it should have at least <n> characters`. The too-long violation in `max_length` uses code 19 and says `it should have no more than <n> characters, but has <m> characters`.

trigger_keywords: string rule message type name
type name in error
which type label is used
integer type label in string error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `string` rule (code=16) includes the detected type in its error message: `Value "<val>" expected to be string, type <typeName> given.` The type label used for a JSON integer is `integer`, for a JSON float is `float`, for a JSON boolean is `boolean`, for null is `null`, and for a JSON array is `array`.

trigger_keywords: date format argument
PHP date format
Y-m-d meaning
date format string interpretation
how to validate dates
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `date` rule (code=214) takes a format string as its first arg using PHP-style date format tokens: `Y`=4-digit year, `m`=2-digit month, `d`=2-digit day. The rule must verify both that the string matches the format pattern AND that the resulting date is a real calendar date (e.g. month 13 or day 99 must be rejected). Error message: `Date "<val>" is invalid or does not match format "<fmt>".`

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

are there public test cases

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: applying a rule across a whole list of values at once
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `all: true` flag on a check-mode request (Feature 9.2 in start.md / feature9_2_all.json) which iterates the rule over every element of the value array

context_pointers.vague_reference: downstream systems can react to specific failure types without parsing human text
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The stable numeric violation codes (e.g. code=9 for float, code=10 for integer) that must be reproduced exactly as specified in the test cases and never changed between versions

context_pointers.vague_reference: both patterns are needed depending on the use case — short-circuit vs collect all
context_pointers.actual_meaning: `chain` mode (stops at first failing step) versus `lazy` mode (evaluates all assertions and aggregates all failures into a numbered report)

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented. Please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected input/output behavior, or refer to the feature descriptions in start.md. If the behavior still isn't clear from those sources, flag it for a team sync.

feature1_1_float.json.description: Accepts values that are real floating-point numbers and rejects every other type, including integers and numeric strings.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=9
message=Value "1" is not a float.

feature1_2_integer.json.description: Accepts native integer values and rejects floats, numeric strings, booleans and null.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=10
message=Value "5" is not an integer.

feature1_3_integerish.json.description: Accepts integers and strings that represent an integer without loss, and rejects fractional values, non-numeric strings, booleans and null.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=12
message=Value "5.5" is not an integer or a number castable to integer.

feature1_4_boolean.json.description: Accepts only genuine boolean values and rejects integers, strings and null.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=13
message=Value "1" is not a boolean.

feature1_5_scalar.json.description: Accepts scalar values (string, number, boolean) and rejects compound values such as arrays.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=209
message=Value "<ARRAY>" is not a scalar.

feature1_6_string.json.description: Accepts string values (including the empty string) and rejects numbers, booleans, arrays and null. The error message reports the actual type that was supplied.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=16
message=Value "5" expected to be string, type integer given.

feature1_7_numeric.json.description: Accepts numbers and numeric strings and rejects non-numeric strings and null.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=23
message=Value "abc" is not numeric.

feature1_8_digit.json.description: Accepts values whose textual form consists only of decimal digits and rejects values containing signs, separators or letters.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=11
message=Value "12.3" is not a digit.

feature2_1_not_empty.json.description: Requires a value that is not considered empty; rejects empty string, zero, null, empty array and false.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=14
message=Value "" is empty, but non empty value was expected.

feature2_2_empty.json.description: Requires a value that is considered empty; accepts empty string, zero, null and empty array, and rejects any non-empty value.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=205
message=Value "x" is not empty, but empty value was expected.

feature2_3_null.json.description: Requires the value to be null and rejects any non-null value, including empty string and zero.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=25
message=Value "" is not null, but null value was expected.

feature2_4_not_null.json.description: Requires the value to be anything other than null; empty string and zero are accepted.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=15
message=Value "<NULL>" is null, but non null value was expected.

feature2_5_not_blank.json.description: Requires a non-blank value: rejects the empty string and strings consisting solely of whitespace.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=27
message=Value "   " is blank, but was expected to contain a value.

feature3_1_min.json.description: Requires a number greater than or equal to a given lower bound; the bound itself is accepted.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=35
message=Number "1" was expected to be at least "5".

feature3_2_max.json.description: Requires a number less than or equal to a given upper bound; the bound itself is accepted.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=36
message=Number "10" was expected to be at most "5".

feature3_3_range.json.description: Requires a number within an inclusive lower and upper bound; values outside the range are rejected.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
code=30
message=Number "0" was expected to be at least "1" and at most "10".

feature3_4_less_than.json.description: Requires a number strictly less than a given limit; equality with the limit is rejected.

cases.expected_output: valid

invalid
